w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: To understand the lifecycle of POSIX threads and implement synchronization mechanisms, such as Mutexes and Semaphores, to manage shared memory and resolve concurrency conflicts (Race Conditions) in a Linux environment.

function call. When you call a function normally, the program waits for it to finish. When you create a thread, the program starts the function in the background and immediately moves to the next line of code.
because the main thread does not wait for other threads unless explicitly told to do so.
To fix this temporarily, we will
thread to wait, giving our new thread enough time to print its message.
thread to reach the CPU and execute its printf statement.
thread to reach the CPU and execute its printf statement.
What happened during those 5 seconds? The main thread is blocked (sleeping), but the process remains
alive, allowing the work thread to finish its task.
Question: Is sleeping for a fixed amount of time an efficient way to synchronize threads? What if the task takes 6 seconds? We will solve this in the next step.
how long a thread will take to finish.

The professional way to synchronize is using pthread_join. This function acts as a "waiting station": it blocks the main thread and prevents it from exiting until the specific thread (in this case, thread1) has finished its execution.
In Step 4: The main thread is now waiting specifically for thread1.
Even though work sleeps for 5 seconds, the program will exit immediately after the "Hello world!" is printed. This is efficient synchronization.
use the getpid() function to find the process ID and a Linux command to list the active threads.
Look at the Process ID printed in your first terminal (e.g., Process ID: 3228). In the new terminal, run the following command (replace 3228 with your actual PID):
identified by the same number as the PID) and the work thread (with a different TID).
One of the main advantages of threads is the ability to launch multiple tasks simultaneously. In this step, we will create two separate threads that execute the same work function.

Note that we now need two pthread_t variables and, most importantly, two pthread_join calls. The main thread will wait for thread1 to finish and then for thread2. If thread1 is already done when the first join finishes, the second join will return immediately.
simultaneously pthread_create(&thread1, NULL, work, NULL); pthread_create(&thread2, NULL, work, NULL);
simultaneously pthread_create(&thread1, NULL, work, NULL); pthread_create(&thread2, NULL, work, NULL);
While the program is running, go to your second terminal. Run the command again with the new
seconds. Does the total execution time of the program become 20 seconds (10 + 10) or stay around 10 seconds?

In a real-world application (like a web browser), one thread might be downloading a file while another is rendering the user interface. To simulate this, we will now assign a different function to each thread.
Operating System will manage in parallel.
Not necessarily. Since they run concurrently and have the same sleep time, the order depends on the OS
In this step, we will write two separate small programs to observe these two different ways of achieving
process pthread_create(&t1, NULL, &routine, NULL); pthread_create(&t2, NULL, &routine, NULL);
process pthread_create(&t1, NULL, &routine, NULL); pthread_create(&t2, NULL, &routine, NULL);
steps, we will prove that Threads share
fork() returns twice (once in the parent, once in the child), whereas pthread_create only starts a specific
In the Thread program, hilos are just execution units within the same process, so
we expect to see the same PID for both.
thread printf("Thread belonging to PID: %d\n", getpid()); return NULL;
'routine' pthread_create(&t1, NULL, &routine, NULL); pthread_create(&t2, NULL, &routine, NULL);
thread printf("Thread belonging to PID: %d\n", getpid()); return NULL;
'routine' pthread_create(&t1, NULL, &routine, NULL); pthread_create(&t2, NULL, &routine, NULL);
ID), as they saw in Step 5 with the ps command.

When a process is forked, the Operating System creates a copy of the parent's memory for the child. This means that variables like x are no longer shared; each process has its own private version of x.
In this step, we will increment x only in the child process (pid == 0). If memory were shared, both would see the change. If it is isolated, only the child will show the incremented value.
Even though x was 2 for both at the start, once the fork() happened, they became two separate "worlds". The child (PID 5002) sees x = 3. The parent (PID 5001) still sees x = 2.

Does moving the variable outside of main make it shared between processes? No. When fork() is called, the entire address space is duplicated. This includes: the Stack (local variables), the Heap (dynamic memory), and the Static/Data segment (global variables).
'x' printf("Process id: %d | Global x: %d\n", getpid(), x);
'x' printf("Process id: %d | Global x: %d\n", getpid(), x);
memory address as the parent, but in a different physical RAM location. Global variables do not facilitate communication between processes.
Processes are like two different computers. Now let's see how threads behave; they are like two people sharing the same desk.
Unlike processes, threads live within the same address space. This means they share the same global variables.
space pthread_create(&t1, NULL, &routine, NULL); pthread_create(&t2, NULL, &routine2, NULL);
space pthread_create(&t1, NULL, &routine, NULL); pthread_create(&t2, NULL, &routine2, NULL);
modifying the exact same memory location that routine2 reads.
Threads are much more efficient for sharing data than processes, but this also makes them dangerous. If two threads try to change x at the same time, you get a Race Condition.
will execute the same routine. In this step, each thread increments the counter only once.
Now, we add a small loop of
to each thread. We want to see if the counter can handle a bit more work.
a longer time, and the Operating System will be forced to switch between them constantly.
changes in every execution because of a race condition. The
operation is not atomic; it requires three CPU steps.
When thread A is at the "Increment" step, the OS might pause it and let thread B "Read" the old value. When
final number is random because it depends on exactly when the OS decides to switch between threads.
To fix the inconsistent results from Step 15, we must ensure that only one thread can access the "Critical Section" (the
line) at a time. We do this using a
lock the same mutex, it must wait until the first thread calls
to release the key. This ensures the
By locking the mutex, we make the three steps of mails++ (Read, Increment, Write) appear as one single, unbreakable (atomic) operation to other threads.
threads are now waiting for each other instead of running blindly.

The power of synchronization with Mutex is that it scales. In this step, we increase the number of threads from 2 to 4. Without a mutex, having 4 threads would make the "Race Condition" even more aggressive and the error in the final count would be much larger.
wait for its turn to increment the counter, ensuring that the final result is exactly 4,000,000.
identifiers pthread_t t1, t2, t3, t4;
routine pthread_create(&t1, NULL, &routine, NULL); pthread_create(&t2, NULL, &routine, NULL); pthread_create(&t3, NULL, &routine, NULL); pthread_create(&t4, NULL, &routine, NULL);
identifiers pthread_t t1, t2, t3, t4;
routine pthread_create(&t1, NULL, &routine, NULL); pthread_create(&t2, NULL, &routine, NULL); pthread_create(&t3, NULL, &routine, NULL); pthread_create(&t4, NULL, &routine, NULL);
Scalability: The logic of the program remains the same regardless of the number of threads. The mutex successfully serializes the access to the mails variable.
even though we are using more "cores." More threads don't always mean more speed if the synchronization overhead is high.

To prove that the mutex was indeed the hero in the previous step, we will now perform a "stress test." By commenting out the lock and unlock commands, we allow all 4 threads to access and modify the mails variable simultaneously without any restrictions.
With 4 threads running 1,000,000 iterations each, the "Race Condition" will be much more severe than it was with only 2 threads.
pthread_t t1, t2, t3, t4; pthread_mutex_init(&mutex,
pthread_t t1, t2, t3, t4; pthread_mutex_init(&mutex,
With 4 threads and no mutex, the corruption of the data is much more evident. The probability of two or more threads reading the same value at the same time increases with every added thread.
Performance vs. Correctness: You might notice the program runs much faster now. However, the result is wrong.
it to block anything; we are just setting up the structure.
// We cast the argument back to int and print it printf("Hello from thread %d\n", *(int*)args); free(args);
// We cast the argument back to int and print it printf("Hello from thread %d\n", *(int*)args); free(args);

malloc in threads: Since the loop moves very fast, if we passed the address of i directly (&i), all threads might end up seeing the same final value of i. We use malloc to give each thread its own "copy" of its ID.
haven't called sem_wait yet, so the threads are printing freely.
Add the sem_wait and sem_post commands to see the difference. Note that the semaphore has a value of 1 set up in sem_init command. Compile and run it.
In computer science theory, semaphore operations are traditionally known by the letters
value is 0, the thread blocks and waits.
and wakes up a waiting thread.
sem_wait(&semaphore): This checks the semaphore's value. Since it is 1, the
thread to arrive decrements it to 0 and proceeds.
Because of the sleep(1), this program will take exactly
forced to go one by one.
identical to a Mutex. It provides Mutual Exclusion.
The real difference between a Mutex and a Semaphore is that a Semaphore can allow multiple threads to access a resource at the same time.
By changing the initial value to 3 in sem_init(&semaphore, 0, 3), you are saying: "There are 3 available
sem_post. Modify semaphores.c and change the initial value:
The last one: The 4th thread had to wait for one of the others to finish (sem_post) before it could print its message.
While a Mutex is like a bathroom key (only one person at a time), a Semaphore is like a restaurant with 3
manage memory. Why is it "easier" but "riskier" for threads to communicate?
Thread Lifecycle: What are the specific roles of pthread_create and pthread_join? What happens to the main program if pthread_join is omitted?
"under the hood" (Read-Modify-Write) and why this matters in multi-threading.
formal definition of a Critical Section. Identify exactly
line of code was the Critical Section in Step 15.
Performance Trade-off: In Step 18, you removed the mutex and the code ran faster but gave the wrong result. Explain the trade-off between execution speed and data integrity.
cast it to (int*), and what does the first * do?
Dynamic Allocation: Why did we use malloc to pass the ID to each thread instead of just passing the address of the loop variable &i?
Mutex vs. Semaphores: What is the main functional difference between a Mutex and a Semaphore?
What happens to the semaphore's internal counter in each?
Counting Semaphores: Compare your observations from Steps 20, 21, and 22. How did changing the initial value (1, 2, and 3) affect the timing and concurrency of the output?

each step followed by an explanation. Every image must be explained.

of the lab and a general objective.
